text: I called Gregory over. “Check.” “Nothing to eat, Sam?” He stood thin and tall, hanging over the table like a bent tree. We’d only had drinks. Bourbon for me. Amaretto sour for Vanessa. “Not tonight.” I ate at The Black Door most weeks, and not because of the name. An old acquaintance, Benny Dickson, owned the place. I’d get comped every so often, but that’s not why I always ate there either. “You promised me dinner.” Vanessa leaned forward and her body did all kinds of interesting things as she pressed against the tabletop. If you guessed I patronized The Black Door because of the food, you guessed wrong. Most dishes were too something . Too greasy, too salty, too fishy. Gregory watched Vanessa wiggle in her seat. He was my waiter. Mine as in Benny hired him for me. Mine as in he was the only person allowed to wait on me. He offered, “How about two usuals to go? I can have it out before you finish your next round.” “I didn’t order—” The bartender appeared and placed our drinks on the table. “As I said. Before you finish your next round.” The man was a pro, but Gregory was also not the reason for my recurring attendance at The Black Door. Vanessa pushed her lush red hair behind her ears. “What’s the usual?” She leaned back. Her summer dress danced across umber skin. “Too spicy.” Our table, my regular table, sat alone in the far corner of the dark dining area. Some would say too dark. I’d heard customers complain that they couldn’t read the menu. That they couldn’t see the food. I’d argue you didn’t want to see the food. “I like spicy.” “Then you’re in luck.” Vanessa peered around the room lazily. “What will we use? If I decide to go home with you, I mean.” “Haven’t you already decided?” Her smile showed plainly now. “You’ve promised a lot. Can you deliver?” I drank my bourbon deliberately and completely. The warmth spread through me. “That depends entirely on you.” Gregory approached and placed a takeout bag on the table. “Your food.” “Almost didn’t make it.” I waggled my empty glass. He dropped another bag onto the table. “Your necessities.” The plastic bag contained a surprising weight and even more surprising contents. This was why I returned to The Black Door time after time, not that I had a choice. “Thank him for me,” I said, meaning it for perhaps the first time since Benny saved my life. As we stood to leave, Gregory placed a skeletal hand on my shoulder. He squeezed too hard and whispered. “At your earliest convenience, Mr. Dickson would like a word.” I buttoned my suit jacket. “Tomorrow.” “Morning.” “Something I should know?” He released his grip and brushed his white shirt smooth. “Bring what you owe.” I took Vanessa’s arm. “Fair enough. Tomorrow morning then.” # I met Benny Dickson in college. Really, I should say we met at college. Neither of us attended Eskuatep, but we were both drawn to the university by the cove, as so many others were. The private institution was home to the country’s strictest admission process, highest price tag, and best doctoral program. Being rich wasn’t enough. Students also had to be geniuses. I wasn’t there for a medical degree. I was an explorer. A journeyman. At least those were the romantic things I told myself. There were stories about the college and the surrounding town. This was before the Internet, when urban legends ran rampant and verification wasn’t always as easy as a few keystrokes. So I left my job as an apprentice fishmonger—one job of dozens from which I’d absconded over the years for similar reasons—and hitchhiked to Delaware. To the university by the cove, where stone walkways led the uninitiated astray, winding baffling routes that to nowhere. When I arrived in Delhyme, the cool sea air hit me with a salty tang. Dusk had fallen, and in a small town like this, few were on the street. The local businesses were closing for the evening, and no one gave me a second look as I vagabonded up the road. So much stonework. Buildings, pillars, statues, cobblestone roads. White rocks weathered with tiny craters or smoothed by rain. Darker boulders acting as natural fences between road and lawn. To find the furniture inside every house was made of rock wouldn’t have surprised me. Creaking shutters from a nearby building aroused my attention. I turned in time to see a light extinguish, and I could feel eyes in the darkness watching. All around me, windows were opened to black and whatever lay beyond. # “ This is your place?” Vanessa’s tone sounded the same as all the others. When I brought women home for the rites, they always expected some dank hovel adorned with animal bones and laboratory equipment. Or maybe newspaper-lined windows and the faint smell of cat urine soaked into filthy carpeting. “It is. Do you like it?” When confronted with my brick townhouse in The Willows, they invariably were surprised. We ascended the staircase to the second floor, all hard wood, marble, and stainless steel. Vanessa gazed around the open floor plan from kitchen to living room, the approval plain on her face. “So you’re, like…rich?” I placed the bags on the kitchen counter. “Comfortable.” She narrowed her eyes. “You’re not at all what I expected from your profile.” “What did you expect?” I worked the knot loose on the plastic bag. “A depraved degenerate.” Her smile suggested she still thought I was. “Whatever gave you that idea?” A clear, gallon-sized plastic container sat inside the bag. I lifted it out. Vanessa watched with limited interest as she removed something from her own bag. She tilted the Bowie knife, catching glints from the rail lighting. “Just in case you don’t have your own tools.” Her teeth showed white and sinister. “We have options, but any blade will do.” I draped
